natural_sources: Human stomach acid (gastric acid is 0.5% HCl)
natural_occurrence.description: Hydrochloric acid is naturally produced in the human stomach as gastric acid, essential for digestion. It also occurs in volcanic emissions and some natural salt formations, but industrial production is entirely synthetic.

manufacturing.description: Produced by dissolving hydrogen chloride gas (HCl) in water. The hydrogen chloride gas is typically generated as a byproduct of industrial chlorination reactions or by directly reacting hydrogen with chlorine gas. Food-grade hydrochloric acid must be of high purity.

other_uses.cosmetics: Rarely used; pH adjuster in some chemical peels (professional use).
other_uses.industrial: Steel pickling, ore processing, chemical manufacturing, pH control in water treatment.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Production of various pharmaceuticals, pH adjustment in formulations.
other_uses.household: Muriatic acid for cleaning masonry, removing rust (industrial grade, not food grade).

eu.conditions: Used strictly as pH regulator; must be neutralized in final product. No residual acid remains free.

usa.notes: Used for pH adjustment in food processing.

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no residue in finished food)

commonly_found_in: Corn syrup and glucose syrup production (processing aid)
fun_facts: The SAME acid your stomach produces naturally for digestion - gastric acid is about 0.5% hydrochloric acid
Your stomach produces about 1.5 liters of hydrochloric acid daily to digest food
When used in food processing, it's always neutralized, so no free acid remains in the final product you eat
Historically called 'muriatic acid' from the Latin 'muriaticus' meaning 'pertaining to brine'
Despite its fearsome reputation, it's actually LESS acidic than your stomach contents
Used more as a processing aid than a food ingredient - helps break down proteins and starches during manufacturing
